data.title: Palantir Cannot Be Caught
data.description:
Palantir Technologies Inc.'s (NASDAQ:PLTR) vertically integrated technology stack is turning data gravity into customer gravity. The company's single Apollo control plain, Ontology layer, and five-day AIP Boot Camps create an increasingly powerful flywheel in which every new workflow increases lock-in for existing customers and reduces friction for future customers. As GenAI explodes, this flywheel is only going to spin faster: Palantir's AIP already powers $1 billion in commercial run-rate, while its 10-year Army framework is worth up to $10 billion. Palantir's growing influence in the commercial sector and increasingly secure position as the nation's mission-critical AI backbone puts the company in an incredibly advantageous position moving forward.

The Architecture That Seeds Network Effects

Ontology: A Common Language for Data and Agents

The core, and perhaps most integral part of the Palantir flywheel, is Ontology. Ontology allows for a live digital twin that carefully maps a customer's workflows, assets, and even people into a common semantic model. What's makes this even more powerful is its ability to be permission-shared across organizations. This means that each complementation contributes entirely new entities, heuristics, and relationships that future customers can inherent and benefit from. Over time, this expanding library of ontologies becomes an unsurmountable moat, as switching vendors would mean recreating years of ecosystem-building and semantic context.

Apollo: Write Once, Deploy Anywhere

Next comes Apollo, which is a single control plane that has the ability to push the same application build to a large network of hosting sites, from hyperscalers like Amazon (AMZN) AWS and Microsoft (MSFT) Azure, to on-prem clusters, disconnected submarines, and even air-gapped intelligence networks. Updates and security patches therefore benefit all environments simultaneously, which only further compounds the value of the installed base.

A Self-Reinforcing Commercial Flywheel

AIP Boot Camps

Palantir's five-day AIP Boot Camp serves as the foundation for the company's go-to-market strategy. Prospective customers enter the boot camp with raw data and often leaves with production-grade workflows. The success of early adopters sends strong signals to future customers that Palantir's platform is enterprise-ready. By showcasing success stories from past AIP Boot Camp with large enterprises like Heineken (HEINY), Walgreens (WBA), and AT&T (T), Palantir only further increases the demand for its commercial business.

Abnormally High Net-Dollar-Retention

Perhaps the single most important metric for Palantir's increasingly strong customer gravity is its reported 128% net-dollar retention. This figure is essentially proof that existing customers are buying more seats, workloads, and AIP agents every year. The company's customer count itself grew 64% YoY to 849, with U.S. commercial revenue growing an astounding 93%. Each expansion feeds into Palantir's increasingly vast Ontology library and helps fund more product R&D, further tightening the loop.

Government Backbone Strengthens the Loop

Palantir's public-sector franchise still acts as the company's backbone and central nervous system. As an example of how much sway Palantir has garnered in Government, the U.S. Army awarded Palantir with a contract worth $10 billion over ten years. Because the same Ontology and Apollo layers also serve classified workloads, the security accreditation earned in the public sector further serves to harden Palantir's products for highly sensitive commercial industries like pharma companies, insurance, banks, and utilities. The additional an of increasingly entrenched public business allows for a two-sided spillover network effect that rivals without deep government ties will likely find increasingly hard to match.

GenAI's Blistering Pace Plays to Palantir's Strengths

A Secular Market That Doubles Every Three Years

GenAI usage is starting to explode across nearly all major domains. Both OpenAI and Anthropic have seen their usage rate approximately quadruple over the past few quarters. Even firms like IDC and McKinsey are estimating are estimating that GenAI will continue to experience explosive growth, with the latter estimating that GenAI could unlock $2.6-4.4 trillion in annual economic value. However, this speed of adoption is expected to race far ahead of governance, with directors ranking data security and model control as primary GenAI concerns.

Why Governance Concerns Favor Palantir

The growing GenAI governance concerns play directly into Palantir's skillset. The company's AIP product offers fine-grained access controls, lineage tracking, and a host of other security features that allow enterprises to embrace GenAI without violating their compliance regimes. As models continue to iterate from Claude's Sonnet-class LLMs to multimodal agents capable of taking over entire workflows, Palantir is able to roll out new model capabilities to multiple environments in a single click, all while preserving lineage and permissions. Therefore, each incremental algorithm increases Palantir's network effects.

Financial Compounding: Network Effects in the P&L

Despite the increasingly absurd expectations surrounding Palantir, the company is somehow still managing to blow out expectations. In Q2, Palantir grew its revenue 49% YoY to $1.004 billion, which beat consensus and prompted management to lift full-year guidance to $4.15 billion. Notably, the company's free cash flow came in at $569 million. This gives Palantir's Rule-of-40 score a 94.

Palantir now has $5.4 billion of cash with zero debt, which means that it can aggressively fund R&D to further push its lead. Every dollar of incremental software revenue drops through at high incremental margins. This translates to an Ontology-Apollo-API network that becomes even more profitable as it scales up.

Competitive Moat and Optionality

The success of Palantir will inevitably draw in far greater competition. Hyperscalers like Microsoft and Amazon are already attempting to move into this space. However, these competitors are still largely offering generic model APIs, unlike the end-to-end operating system that Palantir is able to provide. Moreover, Palantir has cleared facilities and twenty-year battlefield credibility to its name, which acts as a large differentiator. Moving forward, Palantir will be able to monetize on its network in new ways: revenue-sharing marketplaces for third-party agents, usage-based Apollo licensing for non-Palantir workloads, and verticalized "AIP-as-a-Service" bundles. Each adjacent use-case leverages the same core flywheel.

Risks

While Palantir is well on its way to become one of the most transformative companies in the world, its high valuation still presents obvious risks. As such, even a slight slowdown in growth could cause the company's valuation to crash. The company currently trades at a mind-boggling P/E ratio of 577, which is even more stunning for a company already valued at $409 billion. Still, the drivers that could dent demand, like tighter budgets and stricter regulations, also raise the bar for security, auditability and ROI. These are all areas where Palantir outperforms.

Government revenue concentration also presents a large risk. If the U.S. Government decides to pull back on even a few contracts, this could huge a large negative impact on revenue. However, the new ten-year Army contract brings a degree of visibility that Palantir has never had before. Moreover, the company's commercial mix has grown to 44% and will likely continue to grow moving forward.

Conclusion

Network effects are often an overused term in tech, but in Palantir's case, it undersells the magnitude of its flywheels. The company has a growing Ontology that lower time-to-value, an Apollo control plane that can deploy innovation immediately, and a defense franchise that embeds the company inside some of the U.S. most important institutions. The generative-AI gold rush only accelerates these advances since much of Palantir's value-add is dependent upon highly performance underlying models. Palantir is poised to become the operating system of the AI century and is building a wall that competitors will likely find increasingly hard to scale. Even at a valuation of $423 billion, Palantir still has far more room to run.

data.title: Atlassian: Another Dip, Another Chance To Buy
data.description:
We're now in the thick of the Q2 earnings season, and several trends are becoming clear so far in the second half of a volatile 2025. To me, one of the principal shifts is the fact that investors seem to be losing confidence in small- and mid-cap stocks, throwing in the towel and bidding up large-cap winners like Microsoft (MSFT), Palantir (PLTR), and Meta (META).

Investors have less confidence, meanwhile, that AI will also be a benefit to smaller software companies like Atlassian (NASDAQ:TEAM), the collaboration and workflow tool that has lost ~30% of its value since the start of the year - despite Atlassian's agentic AI releases and its strong growth at scale. The company just reported fiscal Q4 (June quarter) results, and investors turned up their nose at Atlassian's guidance for the upcoming FY26, sending shares down further. The question now: Is Atlassian headed for a further correction, or is this dip a buying opportunity?

I think it's the latter. I last wrote a buy opinion on Atlassian in May, when the stock was hovering above $200 per share. Now, it's important to note that I'm not a price-agnostic investor: I was very skeptical on the stock when it was hovering in the high $200s/above $300 earlier this year. But now, with Atlassian having shed ~half of its value in a short amount of time, I believe the market has overreacted to the decelerating-growth narrative that has overtaken this stock. In my view, the stock has become incredibly undervalued, especially for its new suite of AI tools as well as its profit expansion. I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating here and recommending buying the dip while Atlassian is trading at rare low multiples.

As a reminder for investors who are newer to Atlassian, here are the core reasons to be bullish on the stock:

  • Especially as AI expands its use cases, Atlassian is still in the earlier stages of capturing a broad market opportunity. The company estimates its TAM at $67 billion, consisting of a $35 billion market in work management, $17 billion in software development, and $15 billion in IT service management. Its current revenue run rate is still only less than ~10% penetrated into this industry in which Atlassian remains a market leader.
  • Unique sales model leading to rich margins. Atlassian is a true outlier in the enterprise software sector by focusing more on inbound rather than outbound sales. It doesn't have an army of account executives "pushing" its products; rather, it relies on word of mouth and customer expansion. This has allowed Atlassian to build up rich profit margins earlier in its lifespan, which it has sustained at scale.
  • Sky high gross margins. Atlassian's mid-80%s pro forma gross margins are among the highest in the industry, which also allows for significant economies of scale especially with the slimmer spending on sales.
  • Successful subscription transition. Last year, Atlassian killed off support for its final license products, having now successfully transitioned all of its customers into recurring cloud subscriptions, stabilizing its revenue base.
  • Rule of 40. Atlassian combines ~20% growth with a mid-20%s operating margin, putting the stock firmly in the "Rule of 40" category that helps justify a thinning valuation premium.

Speaking of valuation: Despite Atlassian's fundamental strengths, the stock's ~30% slide since the start of the year has nearly tipped the stock into value territory. At current post-earnings share prices near $171, Atlassian trades at a $44.89 billion market cap. After we net off the $2.93 billion of cash and $987.7 million of debt on Atlassian's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $42.95 billion.

For FY26 (which is the fiscal year that Atlassian just kicked off, ending in June 2026), the company is guiding to 18% y/y revenue growth, which implies $6.15 billion in revenue.

We note that part of the reason Atlassian shares tumbled post-earnings is because Wall Street had been hoping for 19% growth for FY26. That being said, we should recognize that Atlassian's guidance philosophy has a bent toward conservatism. For FY25, Atlassian had originally guided to 16% revenue growth but instead, delivered 20% growth. Atlassian is leaving room for "beat and raise" quarters ahead. It's premature to judge the company for its disappointing guidance now.

Still, if we take the current guidance at face value, Atlassian trades at 7.0x EV/FY26 revenue. We note two things: first, despite the fact that Atlassian's pace of growth has barely decelerated (from the ~20%s) over the past few years, the stock has rarely sat at a single-digit revenue multiple. Second, we can stack up Atlassian against a number of similar Rule of 40 software stocks that are growing in the ~20% range like ServiceNow (NOW), JFrog (FROG), HubSpot (HUBS) - and we find that the stock now sits at a substantial discount, especially to the likes of ServiceNow (which actually operates in a very adjacent IT service management space).

Take advantage of this (in my view, temporary) dislocation between fundamental performance and value in Atlassian as a well-timed buying opportunity.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Atlassian's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Atlassian's revenue grew 22% y/y to $1.38 billion, which smashed Wall Street's more conservative expectations of $1.35 billion (+20% y/y) by a two-point margin. Also of note, as shown in the chart below, is the fact that Atlassian's revenue accelerated 8 points y/y versus 14% y/y growth in Q3.

Now, we do note that a large chunk of the acceleration is due to the fact that Q3 had a tougher comp from the sunsetting of Atlassian's server products last year, which spurred a wave of last-time buys in Q3'24. License/server products are now fully out of Atlassian's prior-year compares, putting the focus on the company's cloud revenue streams. Still, we note that while it's not eight points, cloud revenue also accelerated one point to 26% y/y growth in Q4. We note as well that the company's marketplace segment (which features apps by third-party developers intended to integrate into Atlassian systems) rebounded to mid-teens growth.

In particular, we note that AI is helping to drive major wins for Atlassian that we aren't necessarily seeing yet in the company's financials. The company notes that there are now 2.3 million active Rovo AI agents deployed on the Atlassian platform, which increased ~50% sequentially over 1.5 million at the end of Q3. The company also notes that it signed a record number of deals with over $1 million in annual contract value, and that this large-deal count is up 2x y/y over the prior-year Q4.

One of the principal concerns that investors have against Atlassian is the possibility that AI begins to take over more software development jobs. As a work collaboration tool that is aimed primarily at technical users, reduced headcount of software developers would directly impact Atlassian's seat counts and slow down its growth.

When pressed on this possibility on the Q4 earnings call, however, CEO and founder Mike Cannon-Brookes notes that so far, code generation tools have not at all slowed down Atlassian's business or threatened its key customer base:

Let me just start by saying we're not seeing any impact from this on any numbers that we have. So expansion rates, growth rates, adoption rates of our technical products. And again, the technology teams and the software business is a part of our business alongside service strategy and broad collaboration. But we're not seeing any impact from it. So we're being very clear about that. When customers integrate code-generating AI tools, there's no change in their usage of our products, and we continue to see really healthy user growth.

In fact, we even have some early data that shows that when people do connect cogeneration tools, the business users in those geo instances is actually growing faster than they are for the customers that don't have those connected, right, demonstrating, we think, Jira continue to be the center gravity in how work gets done [...]

Software creation is becoming more efficient. Cost of software building going down. This is all really good for Atlassian and our workflow tools. Again, in that software teams and technology teams world that we sit in."

We should also point out that these same trends (fewer software developers and technical users), if true, should also impact other IT management or DevOps tools, such as ServiceNow and JFrog - stocks that are trading at significantly richer multiples than Atlassian.

On the profitability front, Atlassian delivered significant margin expansion as well. As shown in the chart below, pro forma operating margins jumped 470bps yesterday/y to 24.3%:

We note this firmly places Atlassian in the "Rule of 40" category, balancing mid-20%s growth alongside mid-20%s margins. The company's guidance, calling for 24% full-year margins in FY26 alongside 18% revenue growth (which I view to be conservative on both the top and bottom line) also sustains that status.

Key takeaways

To be clear, Atlassian is an investment that has required patience this year, and it will likely be several quarters until the company can regain investors' trust. In my view, the company is well-positioned to execute "beat and raise" quarters in FY26 as its guidance calls for significant deceleration (which plays into investors' fears that AI is a net headwind, not a tailwind to Atlassian). That being said, Atlassian continues to print healthy metrics, including 120% net revenue retention rates that indicate very healthy upsells and spending activity on its platform.

Stay long here and take advantage of discounted multiples to buy.

data.title: Lamar Advertising: Resilient Amid Subdued Marketing Spending
data.description:
Shares of Lamar Advertising Company (NASDAQ:LAMR) have been a modest performer over the past year, gaining 2% alongside their dividend. Ongoing concerns about the state of the advertising market have been a headwind for shares, and these concerns are back to the forefront after a mixed Q2 report on Friday morning. I last covered shares in May, rating Lamar a Buy, and while shares did reach my $125 target, they have since pulled back. As such, the stock has gained just 2% since then, lagging the market’s 8% rally. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit LAMR.

In the company’s second quarter, Lamar Advertising earned $1.52, which beat estimates by $0.07; however, revenue was slightly lower than expectations at $579 million. Lamar generated $225 million in adjusted funds from operations (“AFFO”), up 5.5%. Thanks to share repurchases, AFFO/share was up a faster 6.7% to $2.22. Overall, revenue was up 2.5% from last year, and organic revenue growth was 1.9%.

It has been a difficult advertising market over the past 18-24 months as companies trim budgets amid slowing consumer spending. The added uncertainty and costs from tariffs simply create another reason for firms to further trim their marketing spending. Importantly, Lamar’s business has held up better than other forms of advertising, like cable television. First, billboards do not face the same obsolescence risk as other media forms. There is no “cord-cutting” pressure to driving along the highway and seeing advertisements on billboards.

Additionally, we have generally seen national companies more aggressively curtail advertising spending. Importantly, Lamar has limited national exposure. Local and regional accounts for nearly 80% of the business and grew for a 17th straight quarter. In Q2, local grew by 1.7% with national up 0.5%. Some local spending is nondiscretionary; for instance, you have likely seen a fast-food restaurant advertise that it is located off an upcoming exit to attract visitors. As such, this demand tends to hold up better even during down markets.

On the earnings call, management highlighted relative strength in financial and insurance advertising. Legal has also been a recurring source of strength. Conversely, telecom spending is down 17%, which is a national category. Ongoing “social inflation,” which insurance companies refer to as the increasing litigiousness to pursue claims is likely to be an ongoing benefit while I expect national spending to be comparatively soft with potential incremental pressure from the auto sector.

In this muted environment, Lamar is doing an excellent job controlling costs. Lamar held operating expenses flat to last year at $382 million. As such, adjusted EBITDA grew by 2.5% to $278 million. LAMR generated $199 million of free cash flow, down 2%, given increased capital spending. It remains a highly cash-generative business. LAMR also had $41 million of interest expense, down from $4 million a year ago as floating rate debt benefits from Fed rate cuts over the past year.

Lamar continues to spend to pivot its billboards from traditional static boards to digital ones. Its $38 million in cap-ex was up substantially from $23 million last year as digital spending doubled to $23 million. Within this spending, $13 million was for maintenance cap-ex in Q2, bringing the total to $23 million so far this year. Its guidance still assumes $60 million of maintenance spending.

LAMR is in the process of transitioning boards to digital because they generate much more revenue, given the ability to cycle advertisers. The company has 5,255 digital units, up 152 sequentially. The boards generate about 5x the revenue compared to the prior static board. As it continues this transition, that should be an enduring source of revenue growth, though I would note same-store digital sales were a bit softer in H1 and is a risk to monitor.

Lamar is still in the infancy of this transition, as just 3% of its boards are digital. As you can see, Lamar is far and away the leading owner of billboards with operations across 45 states. This remains a fragmented industry, and Lamar is a consolidator. In July, Lamar acquired Verde Outdoor, which owns 1,500 billboards. It is issuing 1.188 million shares in its operating partnership to the owners of Verde. After one year, they can choose to sell their units, and Lamar can either settle this by issuing to them the equivalent number of LAMR shares or paying in cash the value of that LAMR stock. This values Verde at about $143 million. This transaction increases Lamar’s diluted share count by just over 1%, roughly proportionate to its board count. I do not expect material accretion from the deal.

Importantly, Lamar has the balance sheet flexibility to pursue bolt-on M&A. It has $3.36 billion of debt, and this translates to just about 3.0x debt/EBITDA. Management expects to end the year at or below that level. This is well below its 3.5-4x leverage target, giving it ~$1 billion of potential debt capacity. Given its balance sheet and cash flow, I view its 5% dividend yield as secure.

Now alongside results, management cut its full year AFFO guidance to $8.10-$8.20 from $8.13-$8.28 previously. It is exiting the Vancouver transit market; this has not been a profitable contract but exit costs will be about $0.06 due to severance and other items. This explains most but not all the reduction.

Management said that bookings suggest H2 revenue growth will be slower than initially expected, though it should be positive as it sees demand being a bit softer than it expected and is most cautious starting in October. I would note that it has 91% of Q3 already booked, giving it meaningful visibility to near-term results. Even about 85% of Q4 is booked, so while softening demand may hurt at the margin, LAMR has locked in most of this year’s revenue. Given the relatively tepid economic environment, I expect advertising spending to be flattish over the next year.

Back in May, I was targeting $8.20-$8.28 in AFFO, assuming ~$60 million of maintenance cap-ex. This maintenance figure is in keeping with guidance, though given H1 performance, LAMR could outperform here slightly. Given the Vancouver headwind and slightly higher diluted share count, I now see about $8.12-$8.20.

That still leaves shares with a ~6.9% AFFO yield, which I view as attractive. With a ~1% annual tailwind from digital conversion and ~2% long-term inflation, LAMR is well positioned for ~10% long-term growth with capacity for debt-funded M&A as a further potential catalyst. While the advertising market remains soft, I see LAMR as well-positioned to endure and gain wallet share in this environment, making shares an attractive long-term investment for income-oriented investors.

data.title: MP Materials: A Buy Fueled By Geopolitical Shifts
data.description:
MP Materials Corp. (NYSE:MP) is the largest producer of rare earth materials in the Western Hemisphere. The company’s recent partnerships with the Department of Defense (DoD) and Apple Inc. (AAPL) could potentially boost its production with long-term contracts. I am initiating with a Buy rating with a fair value of $87 per share.

Rare earth elements (REE) contain 17 metallic elements essential for high-tech products and automotive manufacturing. As noted in the 10-K, the neodymium-praseodymium (NdPr) is the largest segment of the REE market, with the market demand growing at a CAGR of 8.4% through 2040, primarily driven by demands for electric mobility, industrial, robotics, motors as well as defense systems. As such, I think it makes sense for the U.S. government to secure its long-term supply of REEs.

In July 2025, DoD and MP Materials formed a public-private partnership to produce rare earth in the U.S. and reduce the reliance on China’s supply. As detailed in the slide below, the DoD entered a 10-year agreement with a price floor commitment of $110 per kilogram for MP Materials’ NdPr products. In addition, DoD will purchase $400 million of MP Materials’ preferred shares with a conversion price of $30.03 per share.

On July 15, 2025, MP Materials announced its long-term partnership with Apple. Under the partnership, MP Materials will supply Apple with recycled rare earth feedstock processed in California. The magnet shipment is anticipated to begin in 2027 and ramp up to support Apple devices’ manufacturing. I view the partnerships with DoD and Apple as transformational for MP Materials, and clearly, the company has been benefiting from the rising geopolitical tensions between the U.S. and China.

To better serve the U.S. market, MP Materials has been working on vertical integration over the past few years, expanding its supply chain, optimizing logistics and increasing talent in engineering and manufacturing areas. For a commodity producer, effective cost management, logistics and supply chain are crucial for the company’s profitability growth, in my opinion.

Before its partnership with DoD and Apple, I have to say the company was not well managed, and very vulnerable to Chinese supplies. As detailed in the table below, while the company increased its NdPr production over the past two years, its NdPr realized price was only $51 per kg in FY24. As such, the $110/kg floor price committed by the DoD is quite a good deal for MP Materials, more than doubling its realized price in FY24.

In addition, due to a lack of scale and low price, the company generated only $203 million in revenue with a $14 million loss in adjusted EBITDA for FY24. Without the U.S. government’s support, the company might be out of business sometime in the future. As such, I believe the geopolitical tensions between the U.S. and China have fundamentally transformed the company.

MP Materials reported its Q2 FY25 result on August 7th, posting 84% revenue growth and 24% adjusted EPS growth. As detailed in the charts below, its rare earth concentrates (REO) production volume increased by 45% year-over-year, and NdPr production increased by 119% with a $57/kg realized price. As noted in the release, the NdPr production increase was primarily driven by the company’s transition to production of midstream products during the quarter. In addition, the company has ceased its REO shipment to China, a strategic transition to the U.S. market.

During its earnings call, the management highlighted its partnership with Apple and the DoD, and discussed its investments in expanding recycling capabilities at Mountain Pass, as well as constructing the 10X Facility, which could boost their manufacturing and refining capacities for rare earth elements in the future.

My key takeaway from the quarter was the management’s commitment to achieving a 6,000 ton per annum NdPr oxide run rate target in the near future. Applying the new $110 /kg floor price, these productions could potentially contribute $660 million in annual revenue. For the upcoming quarter, the company is guiding for a 10% to 20% sequential increase in NdPr oxide production.

Considering its production ramp-up, I estimate the company will achieve 50% annual revenue growth for FY25, driven by growing NdPr oxide production. From FY26 onwards, I anticipate a 30% annual revenue growth, primarily driven by capacity expansions and volume increase. DoD has already provided a floor price and will share 30% of any incremental price above $110/kg. As such, MP Materials will generate limited price growth going forward. I think the U.S. government will actively stockpile its rare earth reserves to reduce its reliance on China.

As the company is expanding its manufacturing and refining capabilities, they will face high depreciation costs in the near term. However, once these facilities are built, the depreciation costs will gradually come down, which could improve its operating margins over time. I forecast the company will deliver 550bps annual margin expansion, driven by 200bps from operating leverage of SG&A expenses, 150bps contribution from depreciation costs and 200bps from operating leverage from their manufacturing facilities. I estimate the total operating costs will grow by 27.3% annually.

With these assumptions, the DCF can be summarized as follows:

I calculate the FCFF as follows:

The WACC is calculated to be 8.6% assuming: risk-free 4.2%; beta 0.8; equity risk premium 6%; cost of debt 5%; tax rate 25%; equity $11 billion; debt $908 million. I set the terminal growth rate to 5% assuming 2% pricing growth and 3% volume growth. Discounting all the future FCFF and adjusting net cash, the fair value is calculated to be $87 per share, as per my estimates.

Key Risks

James Litinsky, the company’s Chairman and CEO, is also the Founder, CEO and CIO of JHL Capital Group (JHL), an alternative investment management firm. I am not comfortable with a CEO running both an investment company and a material company. Similarly, Michael Rosenthal, the COO of MP Materials, used to work for QVT Financial, an investment management firm. When he worked at QVT, he invested heavily in China and global automotive companies. I prefer the senior management team to have more industry-related experience. Lastly, the CFO is pretty young and also worked for an investment company before. It looks like several asset management professionals are running MP Materials.

In the past, MP Materials sold its rare earth concentrates to Shenghe, a related party, and then sold those products to refineries in China. As a result, the company was a purely commodity company without any competitive advantage in engineering and manufacturing for rare earth elements. In addition, Shenghe accounted for 80% of MP Materials’ total revenue.

On July 17th, the company raised $650 million through share offerings at a public price of $55 per share. Considering the current stock price, the share offering is dilutive to existing shareholders. As the company is building its manufacturing capacities, I won’t be surprised if they raise equity in the future. However, considering the DoD’s participation, I don’t worry about MP Material’s balance sheet in the near term. They exited the quarter with around $900 million in total debt, which is a manageable level in my view.

Conclusion

It is evident that the partnerships with DoD and Apple have transformed MP Materials. I am encouraged by the company’s vertical integration and investment in engineering and refining to support future rare earth supply for U.S. customers. I am initiating with a Buy rating with a fair value of $87 per share.
